1.synthesis: Paul, "a prisoner for Christ Jesus on behalf of you Gentiles" (v.1), describes the "stewardship of God's grace" given him: "the mystery was made known to me by revelation" (vv.2–3). The mystery, hidden in former ages but now revealed, is "that the Gentiles are fellow heirs, members of the same body, and partakers of the promise in Christ Jesus through the gospel" (vv.5–6). To Paul, "the very least of all the saints," was given the grace to preach "the unsearchable riches of Christ" (v.8), so that "through the church the manifold wisdom of God might now be made known to the rulers and authorities in the heavenly places" (v.10). In Christ "we have boldness and access with confidence through our faith in him" (v.12).
1.historical_context: Paul writes from prison (likely Rome, c. AD 60–62), and calls himself "a prisoner of Christ Jesus" rather than of Rome (v.1) — reframing shameful chains as honorable suffering for the Gentiles he serves. "Mystery" (v.3) follows Jewish apocalyptic usage (the raz of Daniel 2:28), a divine secret now openly proclaimed, unlike the guarded secrets of the mystery cults. The Old Testament foresaw Gentile inclusion (Isaiah 49:6), but its specific form — Gentiles as co-heirs without becoming Jews — required three newly coined "together-with" words (v.6). Paul's intensified self-deprecation, "the very least" (v.8; cf. 1 Corinthians 15:9), is a recognized rhetorical form of sincere humility. The "rulers and authorities" (v.10) are the cosmic powers who watch the church.
1.literary_structure: This section interrupts the prayer begun at v.1 ("For this reason") with a digression on Paul's apostleship and the mystery, before the prayer resumes at v.14 ("For this reason" again). It expands the "mystery" introduced in Ephesians 1:9 and grounds the Jew-Gentile unity proclaimed in chapter 2. The astonishing claim of v.10 — that the church displays God's "manifold" (literally "many-colored") wisdom to the cosmic powers — makes the reconciled congregation a cosmic exhibit. The boldness and access of v.12 echoes Ephesians 2:18 and leads back into the prayer.
1.theological_application: The gospel makes outsiders "fellow heirs" on equal terms (v.6), a truth that should make the church a community where no one is a second-class member. Paul's self-description as "the very least of all the saints" (v.8) models the humility of those who know grace: the more he grasped Christ's "unsearchable riches," the smaller he became in his own eyes. Most strikingly, the ordinary, reconciled church is God's display of his wisdom to the unseen powers (v.10) — its very existence preaches the gospel to the cosmos — and so the unity of believers is no small thing but a matter of cosmic witness.
1.christology: Christ is the content of the mystery and the treasure Paul proclaims — "the unsearchable riches of Christ" (v.8), inexhaustible and beyond tracing out. The reconciliation of the nations into one body fulfills the promise to Abraham that in him "all the families of the earth" would be blessed (Genesis 12:3) and the Servant's calling to be a light to the nations (Isaiah 49:6), now accomplished through his community. And it is "in Christ Jesus our Lord" and "through our faith in him" (vv.11–12) that believers gain bold, confident access to God — he is the mediator who brings the once-far near.

14.synthesis: Paul resumes his prayer: "For this reason I bow my knees before the Father" (v.14), asking that out of "the riches of his glory" God would strengthen them "with power through his Spirit in your inner being, so that Christ may dwell in your hearts through faith" (vv.16–17). Rooted and grounded in love, he prays they may have power "to comprehend… the breadth and length and height and depth, and to know the love of Christ that surpasses knowledge, that you may be filled with all the fullness of God" (vv.18–19). He closes with doxology: "to him who is able to do far more abundantly than all that we ask or think… be glory in the church and in Christ Jesus" (vv.20–21). The prayer asks for nothing less than to be filled with God himself.
14.historical_context: Paul's note that he "bows the knees" (v.14) is significant, since standing was the normal Jewish posture for prayer; kneeling signals unusual urgency and submission (cf. 1 Kings 18:42). The repeated "riches" (v.16; cf. Ephesians 1:7) draws on the image of a great patron with inexhaustible benefactions. "Rooted and grounded" (v.17) pairs an agricultural and an architectural metaphor for stability, here founded on love rather than philosophical virtue. "Filled with all the fullness of God" (v.19) draws on temple imagery — God's glory filling the tabernacle (Exodus 40:34) and temple — now applied to the believer-as-temple. The doxology (vv.20–21) follows a Jewish liturgical pattern of magnifying God's greatness.
14.literary_structure: The prayer resumed from v.1 forms the climax and bridge of the letter, closing the doctrinal first half (chapters 1–3) before the practical exhortations begin. It rises in a series of escalating petitions — inner strength, Christ's indwelling, comprehension of his love, fullness of God (vv.16–19) — each greater than the last, culminating in the paradox of knowing the love "that surpasses knowledge" (v.19). The doxology (vv.20–21) returns to the "power… at work within us" of Ephesians 1:19, sealing the first half with praise and locating God's glory "in the church and in Christ Jesus."
14.theological_application: Paul's prayer teaches the church what to ask for itself: not merely outward help but inner strengthening by the Spirit so that Christ may truly dwell in the heart (vv.16–17). The goal is to grasp the immeasurable love of Christ — a love so vast it "surpasses knowledge" yet can be experientially known (v.19), the foundation of a stable, rooted life. The doxology lifts struggling believers' eyes to a God "able to do far more abundantly than all that we ask or think" (v.20): our prayers are too small for his power, and the same resurrection power is already at work within his people.
14.christology: The prayer is profoundly Christ-centered: its aim is that "Christ may dwell in your hearts through faith" (v.17), so the indwelling presence of the Lord himself is the substance of Christian strength. The boundless dimensions believers are to comprehend are "the love of Christ" (vv.18–19) — a love displayed supremely at the cross and exceeding all measure. And the closing glory is given to God "in the church and in Christ Jesus throughout all generations" (v.21): the Son is the sphere in which God is forever praised, joining the church to himself as the vessel of God's glory.
